---
title: "3.9 Bank Account Challenge"
subtitle: "this Keyword"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit3-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Groupwork Coding Challenge {.image-fit}

### Bank Account

![Bank Account](../../assets/3-class-creation/3-9-this-keyword/dollarSign.png){fig-align="center" width="18%"}

Many people keep their money in a bank account.

The bank may keep track of the account holder's name, the account balance, and assign an account number to each account.

At the bank, an ATM, or on a phone app, the account holder can deposit or withdraw an amount from the account.

The source ATM video link is omitted; the class design task is retained.

## Pair Work

### For this challenge

You can work in pairs to create a class called `BankAccount`.

The class should keep track of:

::: {.tight-list}
- the account holder's name
- the account number
- the balance in the account
:::

Make sure you use the appropriate data types for these.

## Constructor Requirements

### Use `this`

Write two constructors for the class:

::: {.tight-list}
- one constructor that initializes all the instance variables
- one constructor that only has two parameters for the name and account number and initializes the balance to `0`
:::

For the parameters, use the same variable names as your instance variables.

Use the `this` keyword to distinguish between the instance variables and the parameter variables.

## Method Requirements

### `toString`, `withdraw`, and `deposit`

Write a `toString` method for the class.

Use the `this` keyword to return the instance variables.

Write `withdraw(amount)` and `deposit(amount)` methods for the class.

The `withdraw` method should subtract the amount from the balance as long as there is enough money in the account.

The `deposit` method should add the amount to the balance.

Use the `this` keyword to refer to the balance.

## Test Requirements

### Main method

Test your class with a `main` method that:

::: {.tight-list}
- creates a `BankAccount` object
- calls its `deposit` method
- calls its `withdraw` method
- prints out the object to test its `toString` method
:::

## Coding Challenge

### `activecode:: challenge-BankAccount`

Create a class called `BankAccount` that keeps track of the account holder's name, the account number, and the balance in the account.

Create two constructors using `this`, a `toString()` method, and `withdraw(amount)` and `deposit(amount)` methods.

Use the `this` keyword in the constructor and methods.

Test your class in a `main` method.

## BankAccount Starter

```java
public class BankAccount
{
    // TODO: Add 3 instance variables

    // TODO: Add 2 constructors using this


    // TODO: Add toString method
    // TODO: Add deposit(amount) method
    // TODO: Add withdraw(amount) method
    // TODO: Add main method for testing




}
```

## Test Targets

### Constructor and fields

The Runestone tests check for:

::: {.tight-list}
- `main` method exists and produces output
- 2-parameter constructor passes
- 3-parameter constructor passes
- exactly three private instance variables
:::

The expected output is something like:

```text
Name 101 100.0
Name 101 200.0
Name 101 100.0
```

## Test Targets

### Methods and `this`

The tests also check for:

::: {.tight-list}
- `public String toString()`
- a public `void withdraw` method
- a public `void deposit` method
- at least six uses of `this.`
:::

The source test labels this final check as use of `this.*`.

## Focused Design

### Match source requirements

A complete `BankAccount` class should have:

::: {.tight-list}
- three private instance variables
- one constructor with name, account number, and balance
- one constructor with name and account number, setting balance to `0`
- `toString()` returning all account information
- `deposit(amount)` adding to balance
- `withdraw(amount)` subtracting only when there is enough money
:::

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- use same-name parameters and `this.variableName` in constructors
- initialize balance to `0` in the two-parameter constructor
- keep all three instance variables private
- use `this` in `toString`, `deposit`, and `withdraw`
- prevent withdrawal when balance is not large enough
- test constructor, deposit, withdraw, and `toString` from `main`
:::

## End

### 3.9 complete

Unit 3 strict rewrite is complete through 3.9.
